findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, round or oval mass located within the intraconal space of the orbit, frequently situated retrobulbarly. The lesion typically appears hyperdense on unenhanced CT, and on MRI it demonstrates T1 hypointensity and marked T2 hyperintensity with intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include smooth remodeling or expansion of the adjacent bony orbital walls without aggressive destruction. Occasional punctate internal calcifications or phleboliths may also be present within the mass.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive bone destruction or reactive hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid wing to suggest a meningioma. Furthermore, there is no 'tram-track' enhancement along the optic nerve or multiloculated cystic spaces with fluid-fluid levels indicative of a lymphangioma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intensely enhancing, well-circumscribed, intraconal retrobulbar mass exhibiting marked T2 hyperintensity.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Orbital hemangioma, given the well-defined, intensely enhancing retrobulbar mass with high T2 signal. Cavernous hemangiomas are the most common benign intraorbital tumors in adults and classically present with gradual, painless proptosis.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Meningioma, however optic nerve sheath meningiomas classically exhibit a tubular or 'tram-track' enhancement pattern surrounding the optic nerve rather than a discrete rounded mass. Additionally, meningiomas frequently induce reactive hyperostosis in the adjacent bone, which is absent in this case.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphangioma, given its propensity to present with sudden painful proptosis due to spontaneous internal hemorrhage. However, lymphangiomas are typically poorly circumscribed, infiltrative, multiloculated cystic lesions with characteristic fluid-fluid levels, contrasting with the solid, well-defined nature of a cavernous hemangioma.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the anatomical location of the mass—specifically its relationship to the optic nerve and extraocular muscles—to guide safe surgical excision if symptomatic.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm the slow-growing nature characteristic of a benign cavernous hemangioma and rule out a rapidly expanding malignant process.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits with contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the relationship of the mass to the optic nerve and for demonstrating the characteristic marked T2 hyperintensity and dynamic enhancement pattern of a cavernoma.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted sequences to confirm the hyperintense nature of the vascular spaces, alongside pre- and post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted sequences to demonstrate the intense enhancement.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck / Oculoplastic MDT. My report would clearly document the precise intraconal location of the mass, its size, any mass effect or compression on the optic nerve, and the degree of proptosis to guide definitive surgical enucleation versus conservative observation.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, round or oval mass located within the intraconal space of the orbit, frequently situated retrobulbarly. The lesion typically appears hyperdense on unenhanced CT, and on MRI it demonstrates T1 hypointensity and marked T2 hyperintensity with intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration. Supporting features include smooth remodeling or expansion of the adjacent bony orbital walls without aggressive destruction. Occasional punctate internal calcifications or phleboliths may also be present within the mass. Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive bone destruction or reactive hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid wing to suggest a meningioma. Furthermore, there is no 'tram-track' enhancement along the optic nerve or multiloculated cystic spaces with fluid-fluid levels indicative of a lymphangioma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intensely enhancing, well-circumscribed, intraconal retrobulbar mass exhibiting marked T2 hyperintensity.

The appearances are most in keeping with Orbital hemangioma, given the well-defined, intensely enhancing retrobulbar mass with high T2 signal. Cavernous hemangiomas are the most common benign intraorbital tumors in adults and classically present with gradual, painless proptosis. The principle differential would be Meningioma, however optic nerve sheath meningiomas classically exhibit a tubular or 'tram-track' enhancement pattern surrounding the optic nerve rather than a discrete rounded mass. Additionally, meningiomas frequently induce reactive hyperostosis in the adjacent bone, which is absent in this case. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphangioma, given its propensity to present with sudden painful proptosis due to spontaneous internal hemorrhage. However, lymphangiomas are typically poorly circumscribed, infiltrative, multiloculated cystic lesions with characteristic fluid-fluid levels, contrasting with the solid, well-defined nature of a cavernous hemangioma.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the anatomical location of the mass—specifically its relationship to the optic nerve and extraocular muscles—to guide safe surgical excision if symptomatic. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm the slow-growing nature characteristic of a benign cavernous hemangioma and rule out a rapidly expanding malignant process. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits with contrast if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the relationship of the mass to the optic nerve and for demonstrating the characteristic marked T2 hyperintensity and dynamic enhancement pattern of a cavernoma. I would specify high-resolution coronal and axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted sequences to confirm the hyperintense nature of the vascular spaces, alongside pre- and post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted sequences to demonstrate the intense enhancement. This should go to the Head and Neck / Oculoplastic MDT. My report would clearly document the precise intraconal location of the mass, its size, any mass effect or compression on the optic nerve, and the degree of proptosis to guide definitive surgical enucleation versus conservative observation.
